M4 · Multinomial simplex covariance

JoonHo Lee

2026-07-12

Source: vignettes/m4-multinomial-simplex.Rmd
m4-multinomial-simplex.Rmd

Abstract

For methodologists working on Scenario C (mutually exclusive categories summing to the site-year denominator). Derives the simplex covariance, documents the rank deficiency, and runs the simplex row-sum invariant on a synthetic example.

Overview

This article is written for methodologists working on Scenario C — mutually exclusive categories that sum to the site-year denominator — who need the exact simplex covariance sitemix assembles and why it takes this form. We cover, in order:

  1. the within-site multinomial model an analyst sees and what sitemix encodes;
  2. the local notation this derivation adds;
  3. the simplex covariance and its finite-population corrections;
  4. the rank deficiency and the analytic support rank;
  5. the implementation invariants.

Established vs. novel. Established: the standard multinomial covariance, simplex kernel, and support-rank result (S−1S-1 on positive support) (Agresti, 2013). This package: recording that analytic support rank as matrix_rank, retaining it as structural metadata when a census makes the realized covariance zero, and the diagonal-contract exception for zero-support categories.

Result Attribution
multinomial covariance structure standard (Agresti, 2013)
full-simplex support rank (S−1S-1) standard (Agresti, 2013)
analytic matrix_rank metadata & boundary diagonal exception this package (sitemix)

About the example data. All results here are computed live from alprek_subset, an anonymized, illustrative 50-site sample of Alabama First Class Pre-K records shipped in the package (see ?alprek_subset). It is not a real accountability dataset and must not be cited as empirical Pre-K results. Every code block runs offline with a fixed random seed.

1. What an analyst sees, what sitemix encodes

For Scenario C with KK mutually exclusive categories (e.g., primary language ∈\in {English, Spanish, Other}), the within-site joint distribution is multinomial:

(Cjt,1,…,Cjt,K)∣njt,πjt∼Multinomial(njt,πjt), (C_{jt,1}, \ldots, C_{jt,K}) \mid n_{jt}, \pi_{jt} \;\sim\; \mathrm{Multinomial}(n_{jt}, \pi_{jt}),

with the simplex constraint ∑kπjt,k=1\sum_k \pi_{jt,k} = 1.

2. Notation map (extends M2)

Symbol Meaning Code Range
πjt,k\pi_{jt,k} Category-kk probability theta_raw (per row) ∑k=1\sum_k = 1
Cjt,kC_{jt,k} Category-kk count c_jt_k ∑k=njt\sum_k = n_{jt}
Σjt\Sigma_{jt} Simplex covariance V[[i]]$matrix analytic rank S−1S-1 for positive support SS

3. The simplex covariance

Proposition 1 (Multinomial covariance). For the multinomial sampling model on KK mutually exclusive categories,

Σjt=1njt(diag(πjt)−πjtπjt⊤). \boxed{ \Sigma_{jt} \;=\; \frac{1}{n_{jt}}\bigl(\mathrm{diag}(\pi_{jt}) - \pi_{jt}\pi_{jt}^{\top}\bigr). } \tag{M4.1}

Proof sketch. Direct from the moment-generating function of the multinomial; the variance of Cjt,kC_{jt,k} is nπk(1−πk)n \pi_k (1 - \pi_k) and the covariance of Cjt,kC_{jt,k} and Cjt,k′C_{jt,k'} (for k≠k′k \ne k') is −nπkπk′-n \pi_k \pi_{k'}. Divide by n2n^2 to obtain the covariance matrix of the estimated category proportions. ▫\square

Attribution. Classical multinomial-sampling theory; see Agresti (2013).

Under fixed-population SRSWOR with population size NN, write M=diag(π̂)−π̂π̂⊤M=\mathrm{diag}(\hat\pi)-\hat\pi\hat\pi^\top and define

q={0,N=n,(N−n)/(N−1),N>n. q=\begin{cases} 0, & N=n,\\ (N-n)/(N-1), & N>n. \end{cases}

The first branch includes N=n=1N=n=1. The plug-in and design-corrected whole-matrix rules are

V̂plugin=qM/n,V̂design={0K×K,N=n,(N−n)M/[N(n−1)],N>n and n>1. \widehat V_{\mathrm{plugin}}=qM/n,\qquad \widehat V_{\mathrm{design}}= \begin{cases} 0_{K\times K}, & N=n,\\ (N-n)M/[N(n-1)], & N>n\text{ and }n>1. \end{cases} \tag{M4.2}

The finite-population factors follow standard SRSWOR theory (Cochran, 1977).

The design-corrected rule is selected by bias_correction = “binomial_bc”. Multiplying the whole matrix preserves PSD and the simplex identity V𝟏=0V\mathbf 1=0; a census (N = n) has zero sampling covariance. The covariance metadata records both the conventional FPC and the actually applied plug-in/design multiplier without changing n_eff. Without a supplied finite-population design, the corresponding rules are M/nM/n and M/(n−1)M/(n-1).

4. Rank deficiency

Let S=#{k:Cjt,k>0}S=\#\{k:C_{jt,k}>0\} be the observed positive support. The simplex constraint ∑kπjt,k=1\sum_k \pi_{jt,k} = 1 implies 𝟏⊤Σjt=𝟎\mathbf{1}^{\top}\Sigma_{jt} = \mathbf{0} (the all-ones vector is in the kernel), and every zero-count category contributes a zero row and column. Therefore the analytic simplex support rank is S−1S-1: full support has rank K−1K-1, one zero category has rank K−2K-2, and degenerate support S=1S=1 has rank zero. matrix_rank records this analytic rank rather than a tolerance-dependent eigendecomposition. In a census, the realized sampling covariance is exactly zero but matrix_rank still records S−1S-1; sampling_design and sampling_fraction make that distinction explicit. The constructor validates PSD-ness, the simplex kernel, and the support-rank metadata; no repair is applied in the current Scenario C path.

The sm_vcov constructor records vcov_method = "multinomial" and vcov_scale = "raw". As in Scenario B, the matrix is on the raw scale even when theta_hat is on the arcsine scale; this matches sitemix’s Scenario C contract (Lee, 2026).

For interior categories, the diagonal contract is “row_se_raw_squared”. At a zero-support category, the Wilson scalar surrogate remains positive while the matrix diagonal remains zero to preserve the simplex. That intentional exception is recorded as “row_se_raw_squared_except_boundary_surrogates”. With whole-matrix binomial_bc, V$variance_rule and the applied multiplier record one uniform global design-corrected matrix rule. The zero row and column are unchanged by that scaling, while their row-level scalar provenance remains plug-in or a Wilson surrogate. Validation permits this intentional scalar/matrix provenance mismatch only when the matrix row and column are exactly zero.

5. Implementation invariants

ID Layer Claim
MN1 engine-multinomial Row sums of theta_raw across categories equal 1 (tolerance 1e-12).
MN2 sm_vcov Row sums of V[[i]]$matrix equal 0 (tolerance 1e-12).
MN3 sm_vcov vcov_method = "multinomial" and vcov_scale = "raw".
MN4 sm_vcov matrix_rank = positive_support - 1 (analytic support rank).
MN5 sm_vcov Whole-matrix variance_rule and applied multiplier are uniform.

Verify on a synthetic primary-language slice:

data(alprek_subset, package = "sitemix")
language_data <- transform(
  alprek_subset,
  primary_language = sample(c("english", "spanish", "other"),
                            nrow(alprek_subset), replace = TRUE,
                            prob = c(0.70, 0.25, 0.05))
)
est <- sm_estimate(
  subset(language_data, year == 2024),
  family    = "multinomial",
  indicator = "primary_language",
  vjt       = TRUE
)
V1 <- as.matrix(est$V[[1L]])

# MN1: rows of theta_raw across categories sum to 1 per site-year
per_site <- aggregate(theta_raw ~ site_id, data = est, FUN = sum)
stopifnot(all(abs(per_site$theta_raw - 1) < 1e-12))

# MN2: row sums of Sigma are zero
stopifnot(max(abs(rowSums(V1))) < 1e-12)

# MN3: lexicon
stopifnot(est$V[[1L]]$vcov_method == "multinomial")
stopifnot(est$V[[1L]]$vcov_scale == "raw")
stopifnot(est$V[[1L]]$psd_repair == "none")

# MN4: analytic support rank
stopifnot(est$V[[1L]]$matrix_rank == est$V[[1L]]$positive_support - 1L)

# MN5: one global matrix variance rule and multiplier
stopifnot(length(unique(est$V[[1L]]$variance_rule)) == 1L)
stopifnot(length(unique(est$V[[1L]]$variance_multiplier_applied)) == 1L)

All five invariants hold (1e-12 exact tolerance where numerical).

6. Where to go next

  • M3 · Multivariate SUR covariance — the sibling derivation for Scenario B (overlapping rather than mutually exclusive).
  • M5 · Aggregate engines D0 / D1 for the aggregate-input analog (cross-marginal covariance unidentified). D1 is a multivariate marginal sensitivity path, not aggregate multinomial composition; Scenario C requires student rows or complete sufficient category counts.
  • A4 · Scenarios B / C — multivariate / multinomial for the applied face including the simplex row-sum invariant.

References

Agresti, A. (2013). Categorical data analysis (3rd ed.). Wiley.
Cochran, W. G. (1977). Sampling techniques (3rd ed.). John Wiley & Sons.
Lee, J. (2026). sitemix: Site- and group-level proportions, rates, and sampling uncertainty. https://joonho112.github.io/sitemix/

On this page
